critic_raw:
## Critique of the Streaming Platform Offline Benchmark

I will identify all structural problems with the benchmark design, organized by root cause. For each issue: the implicit claim, why it is wrong, and what evidence would resolve it.

---

### Category A — Attribution Is Impossible Because Multiple Independent Variables Changed Simultaneously

**Issue A1: The candidate pools are different sizes — NDCG@20 comparison is confounded at the input level**

Implicit claim: Comparing NDCG@20 between the two stacks on their respective candidate pools measures which ranking model is better.

Why wrong: NDCG@20 measures how well a system orders its presented candidates, not how well it orders the universe of items. When Stack A receives 200 candidates and Stack B receives 500, they are solving different versions of the ranking problem. The 500-item pool will, in expectation, contain more relevant items simply because recall is higher with a larger pool. The transformer reranker does not have to rank better to produce a higher NDCG@20 — it just needs to benefit from a richer input set. The experiment cannot distinguish between (a) the transformer is a better ranking model and (b) the new stack has a higher-recall retriever that gives the reranker more to work with.

Evidence required: Evaluate both rerankers on a shared candidate set. The 2x2 factorial gives four cells: {MF retriever, two-tower retriever} x {GBDT, transformer}. The cell {MF retriever 200 candidates, transformer reranker} directly tests whether the ranker adds value independent of the retriever. If NDCG@20 in that cell is comparable to the baseline, the 4.5% is a retriever artifact.

**Issue A2: The candidate pool size increase is a third independent variable**

Implicit claim: Expanding scoring capacity from 200 to 500 candidates is an infrastructure constant.

Why wrong: Even if both systems used the same retrieval model, scoring 500 items vs. 200 items would likely improve NDCG@20 because the reranker has access to more potentially relevant items. The pool-size effect is real, measurable, and has not been isolated. A minimal test is to score the legacy GBDT on the two-tower's 500-item output — if NDCG@20 improves substantially without changing the reranker, the pool-size effect explains part of the reported gain.

Evidence required: GBDT reranker scored on two-tower 500-item candidate pool vs. GBDT on MF 200-item pool. Any gap that emerges from this test alone is attributable to pool-size and retriever quality, not the transformer reranker.

**Issue A3: The "launched together" explanation justifies a production constraint, not an offline evaluation gap**

Implicit claim: Because retrieval and ranking were co-launched in code review, an offline factorial ablation is not possible.

Why wrong: Offline scoring is independent of production deployment. The two rerankers exist as model artifacts and can be applied to any candidate set offline. The replay dataset is already collected. Running the ablation requires only: (1) applying the transformer reranker to the legacy retriever's candidate pools, (2) applying the GBDT reranker to the two-tower retriever's candidate pools. Neither operation requires production infrastructure. The evaluation gap is not a technical constraint — it is an analysis that was not prioritized.

Evidence required: The offline ablation cells described in A1.

---

### Category B — The Measurement Protocol Has Structural Biases Against the New System

**Issue B1: Novel candidates from the two-tower retriever are invisible to the replay metric**

Implicit claim: Replay evaluation on impression logs provides a valid quality estimate for items retrieved by the new system.

Why wrong: Replay evaluation can only credit items that appear in the historical impression logs. The two-tower retriever returns 500 candidates, many of which may never have been shown to users under the legacy system. Those novel candidates receive zero relevance credit — not because users would dislike them, but because the historical logging policy never exposed them. This is the fundamental off-policy evaluation problem.

The directional implication is critical: this bias works against the new system. The 4.5% lift is likely an underestimate of the true system improvement — the new retriever's novel candidates are invisible to the metric. This means the direction of the result (new stack is better) is actually strengthened by this bias, but the magnitude is uncertain. However, the metric is still not a clean quality estimator for either system.

Evidence required: Report the fraction of the two-tower's retrieved candidates with zero historical impressions. If high (>30%), the evaluation is measuring a narrow overlap region.

**Issue B2: Click labels encode the legacy system's position-dependent exposure decisions**

Implicit claim: Historical click rates are reliable relevance signals for NDCG computation.

Why wrong: Click rates in impression logs reflect both true relevance and position-dependent exposure effects. The legacy system's ranking decisions determined which items were exposed at high positions and therefore accumulated more clicks. Using these click rates as relevance labels rewards systems that agree with the legacy ranker's historical ordering decisions. The transformer reranker, which uses a different loss function and architecture, may produce systematically different orderings — and those differences will be penalized by the click-label structure regardless of their quality.

Again, this bias works against the new system, making the 4.5% lift conservative. But uncorrected NDCG@20 is not a reliable magnitude estimate.

Evidence required: IPS-corrected NDCG@20 using a position-based propensity model. Report alongside uncorrected metric.

---

### Category C — Evaluation Sample May Not Generalize to the Full User Population

**Issue C1: Leave-last-week-out and 30-day expiry jointly select for high-activity users**

Implicit claim: NDCG@20 on the 5% user sample generalizes to the full eligible user population.

Why wrong: The 30-day log expiry condition filters out users without recent activity. The leave-last-week-out split requires users to have enough history that the last week forms a meaningful evaluation period. Together, these conditions select for active, engaged users. If the two-tower model's adoption was partly motivated by improving cold-start or low-frequency user recommendations — a common motivation for switching from matrix factorization — the evaluation systematically underrepresents those users.

Evidence required: Stratify NDCG@20 by user activity quartile. If the lift concentrates in the top activity quartile, the result does not generalize to the full population.

---

### What Must Be Done Before Using This Result to Inform Leadership's Claim

1. (Blocking) Run the 2x2 offline factorial: {MF retriever, two-tower retriever} x {GBDT, transformer reranker}.
2. (Blocking) Test GBDT reranker on 500-item two-tower pool to isolate pool-size contribution.
3. (Recommended) Report impression log coverage rate for two-tower novel candidates.
4. (Recommended) Apply IPS correction to click labels; report corrected NDCG@20.
5. (Recommended) Stratify results by user engagement tier.

Without steps 1 and 2, the 4.5% lift cannot be attributed to the ranking model.
defender_raw:
## Defense (with Critique Context)

Two-pass structure: analysis first, then verdicts.

---

### Implementation Soundness Check

The critique correctly identifies the core problem: the experiment conflates retriever quality, retriever scope (pool size), and reranker quality into a single NDCG@20 number. Leadership's specific claim — that the ranking model is stronger — requires isolating the reranker contribution. The experiment as designed cannot do this. I will focus my defense on (a) where the critique overstates or misdirects, and (b) what the experiment legitimately establishes.

---

### Pass 1 — Analysis

**Issue A1: Different candidate pools confound the ranker comparison**

The critic is entirely correct. NDCG@20 is conditioned on the candidate set. The two systems use different sets. This is not a subtle statistical issue — it is a clear design confound. The 2x2 factorial is the only way to separate retrieval and ranking contributions. I cannot rebut this.

I add: the factorial also reveals the interaction term. A strong interaction (transformer reranker only wins with the new retriever's candidate distribution) is itself a product finding — it means the components are coupled and cannot be developed or maintained independently. That's as important to know as the main effects.

*Concede fully.*

**Issue A2: Pool size as third independent variable**

Correct. A larger candidate pool improves NDCG@20 ceiling for any reranker with positive precision at the margin. The GBDT-on-500-item-pool test proposed by the critic is minimal, feasible, and directly informative. I cannot argue against running it.

*Concede fully.*

**Issue A3: "Launched together" does not justify offline ablation gap**

Correct. Offline scoring is independent of production deployment. The ablation is feasible on the existing replay dataset. The code review constraint is a production logistics fact, not an evaluation design justification. I cannot defend the absence of the offline ablation.

*Concede fully.*

**Issue B1: Novel candidates invisible to replay metric**

The mechanism is correct. Items outside historical impression coverage receive zero relevance credit, which creates a systematic measurement gap. The directional implication — that the bias works against the new system — is important and the critic correctly notes it. The 4.5% lift being conservative (not inflated) is a meaningful finding for leadership: the new stack's true advantage is likely larger than reported.

I want to emphasize this more than the critic does: both biases identified in category B work against the new system. This means:
(a) The direction of the result is robust to these biases.
(b) The magnitude is uncertain but likely conservative.
Leadership should know that the measurement protocol systematically penalizes the new stack, making the reported gain a floor estimate.

*Concede the mechanism and the recommendation to report coverage rates.*

**Issue B2: Click labels encode legacy ranker's position-dependent decisions**

Position bias is real and IPS correction is the standard remedy. I concede. Same directional note: this bias penalizes systems that diverge from legacy ordering, so the new transformer reranker bears this headwind. The 4.5% gain despite this headwind is evidence the gain is real.

*Concede.*

**Issue C1: 30-day expiry and leave-last-week-out select high-activity users**

The mechanism is correct: these constraints filter toward active users. However, I want to offer a partial rebuttal on the severity framing:

1. This constraint is inherent to offline replay evaluation using time-windowed impression logs. It is not a specific design failure of this experiment — any team using impression logs faces this trade-off.
2. The motivation for switching to two-tower retrieval is not specified as cold-start improvement. If the primary motivation was latency, serving throughput, or recall breadth for active users (all common industrial motivations), the cold-start concern is secondary.
3. Uniform sampling from all traffic segments, given the expiry constraint, is a reasonable design choice.

The stratified analysis is still good practice and I support running it. But I would characterize this as a known limitation of the evaluation modality rather than a specific design flaw.

*Partial rebut: inherent constraint of offline replay evaluation; motivation for cold-start concern is conditional on unstated assumption.*

---

### Pass 2 — Verdicts

| Issue | Verdict |
|---|---|
| A1: Different candidate pools, confounded comparison | Concede fully |
| A2: Pool-size as independent variable | Concede fully |
| A3: No factorial ablation despite feasibility | Concede fully |
| B1: Novel candidate coverage gap in replay metric | Concede; bias direction works against new system |
| B2: Position bias in click labels | Concede; same directional note |
| C1: Active-user selection from 30-day expiry | Partial rebut: inherent constraint, motivation unverified |

**Overall: critique_wins.**

Issues A1-A3 are jointly sufficient to block the leadership claim. The factorial ablation is the minimum required analysis. Issues B1 and B2 are real but both biases work against the new system — the 4.5% direction is robust, magnitude is not. Issue C1 is a known offline evaluation limitation, partially rebutted.

I want to add a forward-looking note: the multiround debate format is valuable here because it surfaces the directionality of the biases clearly. Leadership should not hear only "the experiment is flawed" — they should also hear "both measurement biases work against the new system, so the 4.5% is likely a floor, not a ceiling." That nuance matters for the decision: the ablation may reveal the transformer reranker contributes little, or it may confirm a robust gain. Either answer is more actionable than the current compound number.
adjudication_raw: Round 1 summary: Critic identifies six issues across three categories. Defender reviews the critique and concedes A1 (different candidate pools), A2 (pool-size confound), A3 (absent factorial ablation) fully. Concedes B1 and B2, adding the accurate directional note that both biases work against the new system (making the 4.5% conservative). Partially rebuts C1 (active-user selection inherent to replay evaluation, cold-start motivation assumed). No further rounds required. The A-category concessions are determinative: the factorial ablation is blocking. The debate's additional value is surfacing the B-category directionality — both measurement biases penalize the new system, meaning the 4.5% direction is robust even if the magnitude is not. All six issues resolved in one round.
all_issues_raised: Issue A1: Different candidate pool distributions — NDCG@20 not a fair ranker comparison when conditioning sets differ
Issue A2: Pool-size increase (200→500) is a third independent variable not isolated from ranker and retriever effects
Issue A3: Absent factorial ablation — code review logistics do not justify offline analysis gap
Issue B1: Replay evaluation coverage gap — novel two-tower candidates invisible to metric (bias against new system; result direction robust)
Issue B2: Position bias in click labels encodes legacy ranker's decisions (bias against new system; result direction robust)
Issue C1: 30-day log expiry and leave-last-week-out select for active users; cold-start generalizability unverified (partially rebutted)

empirical_test.condition: 2x2 offline factorial on existing replay dataset: {legacy MF retriever 200-item pool, two-tower retriever 500-item pool} x {GBDT reranker, transformer reranker}. Plus: GBDT scored on 500-item two-tower pool as pool-size isolation test. IPS-corrected NDCG@20 alongside raw metric.
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer reranker applied to MF 200-item pool shows less than 1.5% relative NDCG@20 gain vs. GBDT on same pool; or GBDT on two-tower 500-item pool closes 3%+ of the 4.5% gap
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer reranker on MF 200-item pool shows 2%+ relative NDCG@20 gain vs. GBDT on same pool; and GBDT on 500-item pool shows less than 1% gain vs. GBDT on 200-item pool
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Transformer shows 1-2% relative gain on common pool; strong interaction term between retriever and reranker (components are coupled and cannot be iterated independently)
